academic_definition: An experiment that removes or changes parts of a system to estimate each part's contribution.
plain_explanation: Researchers disable one ingredient at a time to see what actually helps.
analogy: Remove one ingredient from a recipe and compare the result.
when_to_use: When explaining evidence about which method components matter.
common_misunderstanding: It shows association within the tested setup, not that a component is universally necessary.

academic_definition: A mechanism that computes context-dependent weights over representations when producing another representation.
plain_explanation: The model assigns more influence to information that seems relevant at the current step.
analogy: While answering a question, a reader looks back at the most relevant sentences.
when_to_use: When a model selectively combines tokens, image regions, or other features.
common_misunderstanding: Attention weights are not automatically reliable explanations of model reasoning.

academic_definition: An efficient application of the chain rule that computes gradients through a computational graph.
plain_explanation: It traces prediction error backward through the network to determine how each parameter should change.
analogy: Tracing a wrong total backward through a spreadsheet to find each cell's influence.
when_to_use: When explaining how neural networks learn from errors.
common_misunderstanding: Backpropagation computes gradients; an optimizer uses them to update parameters.

academic_definition: A reference method or result against which a proposed approach is compared.
plain_explanation: It is the existing yardstick used to judge whether the new method helps.
analogy: Comparing a new route with the usual route before calling it faster.
when_to_use: When describing comparative experiments.
common_misunderstanding: A weak or poorly tuned baseline can make an improvement look larger than it is.

academic_definition: A standardized dataset, task, metric, or suite used to compare systems.
plain_explanation: A shared test lets researchers compare models under roughly common rules.
analogy: A common exam taken by students from different schools.
when_to_use: When explaining standardized evaluation.
common_misunderstanding: High benchmark scores do not guarantee strong real-world performance.

academic_definition: A neural network that applies learned local filters with shared parameters across spatial positions.
plain_explanation: It scans an image for reusable local patterns such as edges and shapes.
analogy: Using the same small stencil across every part of a picture.
when_to_use: When explaining vision models built from convolutions.
common_misunderstanding: CNNs do not inherently understand objects as humans do.

academic_definition: A learned numerical vector representing an item so useful relationships can be encoded geometrically.
plain_explanation: It turns an item such as a word or image into coordinates that a model can compare.
analogy: Placing books on a map where similar books sit near one another.
when_to_use: When discussing learned representations or similarity search.
common_misunderstanding: Closeness reflects training objectives and data, not objective meaning.

academic_definition: One complete pass through the available training examples.
plain_explanation: The model has had one opportunity to train on every example in the training set.
analogy: Reviewing every card in a study deck once.
when_to_use: When describing training duration.
common_misunderstanding: More epochs can cause overfitting rather than continuous improvement.

academic_definition: Continuing training of a pretrained model on data or objectives tailored to a target use.
plain_explanation: A broadly trained model receives additional practice for a more specific job.
analogy: A general doctor completing specialist training.
when_to_use: When a pretrained model is adapted to a task or domain.
common_misunderstanding: Fine-tuning does not guarantee removal of biases or factual errors.

academic_definition: An iterative optimization method that updates parameters in a direction intended to reduce an objective.
plain_explanation: The training process repeatedly adjusts settings toward lower measured error.
analogy: Taking downhill steps while trying to reach a valley.
when_to_use: When explaining parameter optimization.
common_misunderstanding: It may reach a useful solution without finding the globally best one.

academic_definition: Generated content that is unsupported by the input, evidence, or established facts while appearing plausible.
plain_explanation: The model confidently produces information that is not grounded in a reliable source.
analogy: Giving a polished answer after guessing missing details.
when_to_use: When discussing factual reliability of generative models.
common_misunderstanding: Fluent wording is not evidence that a generated claim is true.

academic_definition: A language model with many learned parameters trained on large text collections, commonly using a Transformer architecture.
plain_explanation: It learns statistical patterns in large amounts of text to predict and generate language.
analogy: An extremely broad autocomplete trained on many kinds of writing.
when_to_use: When explaining modern text generation systems.
common_misunderstanding: Generating convincing language does not imply human-like understanding or dependable knowledge.

academic_definition: A function that quantifies discrepancy between a model's outputs and a training objective.
plain_explanation: It gives the model a numerical signal for how wrong its output was.
analogy: A score that penalizes misses more than accurate shots.
when_to_use: When explaining what training attempts to minimize.
common_misunderstanding: A lower training loss does not necessarily mean better real-world performance.

academic_definition: A condition in which a model fits training data patterns that do not generalize to new data.
plain_explanation: The model learns the practice questions too specifically and performs worse on new ones.
analogy: Memorizing an answer key instead of learning the subject.
when_to_use: When training performance exceeds validation or test performance.
common_misunderstanding: A complex model is not necessarily overfit; the issue is poor generalization.

academic_definition: Among examples predicted positive, the fraction that are truly positive.
plain_explanation: Of everything the model flagged, precision measures how much was actually relevant.
analogy: Of all alarms sounded, how many corresponded to real fires.
when_to_use: When false positives matter.
common_misunderstanding: Precision does not measure how many true positives the model missed.

academic_definition: Among truly positive examples, the fraction correctly predicted positive.
plain_explanation: Of everything that should have been found, recall measures how much the model found.
analogy: Of all real fires, how many triggered an alarm.
when_to_use: When false negatives matter.
common_misunderstanding: High recall can coexist with many false alarms.

academic_definition: A learning framework in which an agent acts in an environment to maximize expected cumulative reward.
plain_explanation: An agent learns behavior through feedback from the consequences of its actions.
analogy: Learning a game by trying moves and receiving scores.
when_to_use: When decisions affect future states and rewards.
common_misunderstanding: The reward definition may encourage unintended behavior.

academic_definition: Learning representations from supervision signals constructed from the data itself.
plain_explanation: The model creates practice tasks from unlabeled data, such as predicting hidden text.
analogy: Covering words in a book and testing yourself on them.
when_to_use: When describing pretraining without manually supplied labels.
common_misunderstanding: It still depends on choices about data and training objectives.

academic_definition: A discrete unit produced by tokenization and processed by a language model.
plain_explanation: Text is split into model-readable pieces that may be words, word parts, or punctuation.
analogy: Breaking a sentence into tiles before arranging it.
when_to_use: When explaining language model inputs, outputs, or context length.
common_misunderstanding: One token is not always one word.

academic_definition: A neural architecture built around attention and position-wise transformations for processing sequences or sets.
plain_explanation: It lets each input piece combine information from other relevant pieces, often in parallel.
analogy: A group discussion where each participant consults the most relevant others before updating notes.
when_to_use: When explaining most modern LLMs and many vision or multimodal models.
common_misunderstanding: Transformer is an architecture family, not a guarantee of capability or reliability.
